topic: Solar Diameter: 52% Variation Through the Day — self-contradiction section (post-EXP-100 rewrite)
our_argument_summary: Section 2.4 argues that a local sun at ~5,733 km orbiting the dome disc at ~7,400 km radius would produce a 52% angular diameter reduction over the course of a day, while measured solar diameter is constant to 0.01%. The section adds three new arguments compared to C1: (1) precise geometry with distances at 60° and 90° hour angles, (2) the W021 smoking gun (dome predicts moon diameter variation but omits the corresponding solar test), and (3) preemptive closure of the aetheric refraction escape hatch. Cross-references Section 7.10 for the full observational analysis.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No dome calculation pipeline for solar angular diameter exists. monitor.py has no domain for solar diameter checks. The dome's predictions page has W021 for moon angular diameter (>2% variation) but no solar analog.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: N/A — section review, not WIN review
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: AI Context Rule 11: 'The 5,733 km Sun altitude is an OPTICAL illusion from crepuscular ray convergence.' The dome's pre-built defense against angular diameter arguments. Section now addresses this directly in the fourth paragraph.

holes_found.description: MID-AFTERNOON MISLABELING FOR 52%. Both the TLDR and the body text claim '52% reduction between noon and mid-afternoon.' The 52% figure derives from the 90° hour angle calculation (6 hours after noon = 6 PM). Mid-afternoon conventionally means 2–4 PM (2–4 hours after noon). At true mid-afternoon (3 PM, 45° hour angle), the dome geometry predicts only ~29% reduction. At 4 PM (60°), it's ~39%. The 52% requires 6 hours, which is early evening, not mid-afternoon. A dome defender could legitimately say we're overstating the effect at mid-afternoon by 23 percentage points, or don't understand our own calculations. The section heading ('52% Variation Through the Day') is accurate — the mismatch is in the TLDR and the body's concluding sentence.
holes_found.recommendation: Change 'between noon and mid-afternoon' to 'between noon and late afternoon' or 'over the course of the afternoon' in both the TLDR and the body. Alternatively, reframe: 'By 4 PM (60° hour angle), the sun should already appear 39% smaller; by 6 PM the reduction reaches 52%.' This gives a progressive picture and avoids the misleading label. The heading '52% Variation Through the Day' is fine.

holes_found.description: SUN ORBITAL RADIUS UNSOURCED IN THIS SECTION. The text states 'orbiting the pole at radius ≈ 7,400 km' without explaining where this number comes from. Section 7.10 clarifies it as 'the Tropic of Cancer in dome coordinates,' but Section 2.4 does not. A dome defender unfamiliar with section 7.10 would ask: 'Where did you get 7,400 km? Our model doesn't state a sun orbital radius.' The value is approximately correct for the Tropic of Cancer under simplified AE projection (assuming equator at ~10,000 km from pole, Tropic of Cancer at ~7,389 km), but this derivation is never shown.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a parenthetical: 'orbiting the pole at radius ≈ 7,400 km (the Tropic of Cancer in dome coordinates — the most favorable case for the dome, as the equinox orbit at ~11,000 km would produce even greater variation).' This both sources the number and notes it's a steelman.

holes_found.description: CROSS-REFERENCE TO SECTION 7.10 IS IMPRECISE. The link text says 'Section 7.10' but the href is '#part7' with onclick showTab('falsify'), which navigates to the top of Part 7 rather than to the specific Section 7.10 heading. A reader clicking through lands at the Part 7 introduction and must scroll to find 7.10.
holes_found.recommendation: Change the href to '#p7-710-9' (the actual anchor ID of Section 7.10) so clicking navigates directly to the relevant section.

holes_found.description: SECTION 2.4 AND 7.10 SHARE NEAR-IDENTICAL CONTENT. The C1 review flagged redundancy between these sections (then numbered 4.5.4 and 4.8). The rewrite makes them more similar, not less: both now use the same numbers (5,733 km, 7,400 km, 9,360 km, 11,930 km), the same W021 argument, and the same refraction escape-hatch text. Section 2.4 is framed as 'self-contradiction' and 7.10 as 'observational test,' but the overlap is substantial. A hostile reviewer could say we're padding our section count. However, this is a structural/editorial concern rather than a factual error, and the two framings (internal contradiction vs. external test) do serve different rhetorical purposes.

stronger_arguments.description: REFRACTION SELECTIVITY PARADOX — The section notes the dome omits a solar diameter prediction while registering W021 for the moon, but doesn't drive home the logical contradiction with the refraction defense. If aetheric refraction preserves the sun's angular constancy (paragraph 4's defense), then the same refraction should also preserve the moon's angular constancy — meaning W021 should predict NO moon diameter variation. But W021 predicts >2% variation, implying refraction does NOT perfectly compensate geometric distance changes. The dome cannot invoke refraction to save the sun's constancy while simultaneously predicting the moon shows geometric variation. This is a true dilemma: either refraction compensates (and W021's prediction is wrong by the dome's own physics) or it doesn't (and the sun should shrink 52%). Making this explicit would connect paragraphs 3 and 4 into a single devastating argument.

OBSERVER POSITION SENSITIVITY — The section assumes the observer is directly beneath the sub-solar point at noon (most favorable case giving maximum variation). For observers at other latitudes, the noon sun is already at a slant, reducing the fractional distance change. But even at the least favorable case (observer far from sub-solar latitude), the predicted variation is still enormous compared to 0.01%. Noting this briefly ('Even for observers far from the sub-solar latitude, where the noon distance is already large, the predicted variation exceeds 15%') would preempt the defense that the calculation only works at one special location.

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome model's intuition that atmospheric/medium effects can alter apparent angular size is physically correct. Real atmospheric refraction distorts the sun's apparent shape near the horizon — the vertical diameter compresses by ~0.5% from differential refraction. The concept of a medium between observer and object affecting apparent size has genuine physical grounding.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Real atmospheric refraction produces ~0.5% vertical distortion near the horizon, not 52% isotropic size preservation across the entire sky. The dome needs a correction factor that precisely replicates distant-sun geometry (1/cos(θ) scaling) at all positions, all times, all seasons — to 0.01% precision. No specified mechanism, no formula, no independent measurement. Furthermore, if such refraction existed, it should affect both the sun and the moon identically, contradicting W021.

text_fingerprint.section_title: 2.4 Solar Diameter: 52% Variation Through the Day
text_fingerprint.heading_text: 2.4 Solar Diameter: 52% Variation Through the Day

deeper_analysis:
The EXP-100 rewrite is a substantial improvement over the C1 text. All major C1 holes are addressed: the sun altitude is now correct (5,733 km, not 8,537 km), the W021 smoking gun is prominently featured, the refraction escape hatch is preemptively closed, citations are specific (Greenwich 1836-1953, SDO/HMI), and the quantitative geometry is precise.

The remaining issues are execution-level, not argument-level. The most concerning is the 'mid-afternoon' mislabeling for the 52% figure. In isolation this is merely imprecise, but it appears in both the TLDR (the first thing readers see) and the body's concluding sentence. A dome defender who does the math will correctly point out that at true mid-afternoon (3 PM), the predicted reduction is ~29%, not 52%. We'd still win the argument (29% is still 2,900× larger than observed), but the imprecision undermines credibility — exactly the kind of sloppiness we criticize the dome model for.

The strongest unrealized argument is the refraction selectivity paradox. Currently, paragraphs 3 (W021 smoking gun) and 4 (refraction escape hatch) are presented as separate arguments. But they're actually two prongs of a single dilemma: either refraction compensates geometric distance effects (in which case W021 is wrong — the moon shouldn't show variation either) or it doesn't (in which case the sun should shrink). Connecting these two points explicitly would make the argument truly airtight.

The redundancy with Section 7.10 remains substantial. Post-rewrite, both sections use identical numbers, identical citations, and the same W021 argument. The framing differs (2.4 = self-contradiction, 7.10 = observational test), but a reader who encounters both will notice extensive overlap. This isn't a factual problem — both sections are correct — but it's an editorial concern that a hostile reviewer could use to claim we're padding.

Overall: the rewrite is solid. The core argument was always devastating; the execution now matches the strength of the underlying logic. Minor edits to fix the 'mid-afternoon' label and strengthen the refraction/W021 connection would bring this to full rigor.

citations_verified: Sun altitude 5,733 km: confirmed in raw-text/05-model.txt line 38
Sun altitude < 4,200 km (physical): confirmed in raw-text/05-model.txt line 39
AI Context Rule 11 ('optical illusion'): confirmed in raw-text/07-ai-context.txt line 24
W021 ('Moon angular diameter variation: >2% moonrise vs transit'): confirmed in raw-text/03-predictions.txt line 61
Moon altitude 2,534 km: confirmed in raw-text/05-model.txt line 41
Geometric calculation: √(5733² + 7400²) = 9,361 km ≈ 9,360 km — verified by independent computation
52% reduction at 90° hour angle: distance ratio 11,933/5,733 = 2.081; reduction = (1 - 1/2.081) × 100 = 51.9% ≈ 52% — verified
citations_unchecked: Royal Observatory Greenwich micrometer measurements 1836–1953: plausible (the Royal Observatory did operate a solar department), but no specific DOI or publication cited in the section.
SDO/HMI 2010–present: The Solar Dynamics Observatory launched Feb 2010, and HMI has been operational since. Plausible but no specific dataset or DOI cited.

Solar diameter constancy to 0.01%: This figure represents daily variation. The annual variation of ±1.67% from eccentricity is approximately correct (e = 0.0167, total range ≈ 3.3%). The <0.01% daily constancy claim is plausible for above-atmosphere measurements but would need citation for ground-based measurements, where atmospheric refraction introduces visible distortion near the horizon.
